item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY 2.0
Title: X-fois gens chaise, coin St-Denis et Ste-Catherine 1
Author: art_inthecity
Release year: 2010

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article; it's short, sweet, and it goes straight to the point. Even though it's short, the anomaly's characteristics are still made known.
+1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I like these.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a chance to talk to Roget about this piece, and his headcanon surrounding it. It was during this conversation that the idea to link our wayward tourist here to SCP-1841 arose, as its possible that the documents left behind by SCP-1996 could very well be pages of the next "1001 Places to See Before You Die". As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Occasionally, documents will be left behind by SCP-1996. Documents of this nature appear to relate the area SCP-1996 had appeared in, presented in the form of a tourist's guidebook. As of 11/12/1999, over 200 individual documents relating to SCP-1996 have been recovered.

To this:

Occasionally, documents will be left behind by SCP-1996. Documents of this nature appear to relate the area SCP-1996 had appeared in, presented in the form of a tourist's guidebook. As of 11/12/1999, over 200 individual documents relating to SCP-1996 have been recovered, several of which make reference to a "Periscope Publishing"1.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Investigation has shown that the name was registered in 1985; however, no books were ever published under the label and it is considered defunct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 5: So.. the point of the article is that it actually appears in random places, but the researcher is trying to draw references to famous things to make the locations it appears in significant, but there's actually no particular pattern at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a perfectly serviceable conceit, and the execution hits enough of the right notes to make for a good read. I upvoted. Some minor typographical errors and tone fixes that may or may not be useful:

+ show block
– hide block

walking through stairs which did not exist prior to SCP-1996's appearance

Obviously it's possible that this is intentional, but: Walking through stairs? "Traversing stairs" would be an appropriate substitute, if that was not your meaning.

Attempts to meet SCP-1996 with hostility will result in it dematerializing.

"Its dematerialization" is more neutral with regard to assuming agency on the apparition's part, and fits more neatly with its ambiguous description as a "phenomenon" elsewhere in the piece.

"It(s) all beautiful, isn't it?"

I assume that what the entity is in fact saying verbatim is "It all beautiful, isn't it?" Assuming that, the parenthetical correction needs an apostrophe. Alternatively, transcribe it as is and bookend with a [sic] to indicate the intention here.

Documents of this nature appear to relate the area SCP-1996 had appeared in

Dropped a "to" after "relate", there, and can afford to drop the "had".

the amusement park constructed in the city of [REDACTED]

"An" amusement park would be more appropriate.

which was home to the third cousin of renown poet

Should be "renowned" instead of "renown", here.

Has the distinction of being the first Foundation facility to be built in the 20th Century

Century doesn't need to be capitalized. Also, this sentence needs a period at the end.

SCP-1996 phenomenon consists of it appearing at historical

Silverlighted raises a good point with the awkwardness of that sentence. This ties into a broader tone issue with the article: You refer to this as "SCP-1996" constantly throughout, which by my understanding of the guidelines is a no-no. Referring to it as "the phenomenon", "the subject", "the entity", so forth, is both more clinical and (more importantly) could help to obviate the need for somewhat clunky constructions like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does it only manifest in the United States?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoting because the documents are great.

However, this piece still displays an overall lack of interesting detail.

If a subject attempts to speak to SCP-1996, it will respond with some variant of "It(s) all beautiful, isn't it?"

C'mon. Can't we get more than just this? Seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just feels like it's building up for something and never gets there. Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few things seem awkwardly warded (but I'm still newer and still trying to get the hang of this site's tone), such as "SCP-1996 phenomenon consists of it appearing". The docs read kinda weird too…but that might be intentional, which I accept.

Anyways, I really liked this one. I really want more addenda…but that's just my being greedy. I disagree with a previous comment I say…I feel like this article isn't just a "let's say creepy and weird things". There's a premise here, and it's a creative one (and judging from the comments, I'm assuming it hasn't been done.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hunter S. Thompson does Lonely SCPlanet. This should be awesome. With so few examples of the "tour guide", it's not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a tourist that shows up randomly and leaves strange booklets? I'm not feeling it… sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I don't like using "appears", so consider that a stylistic choice.

2. I refer to all human/human looking SCP's as humanoid, even if we know they're humans.

3. There is a picture. I figured that would answer questions of that nature.

4. That's what the addendum is for.

5. thatsthepointofthearticle.jpeg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1996 is a humanoid organism, approximately 1.4 meters in height with an unknown weight.

1) Appears to be a humanoid organism. Or do they somehow know it's an organism?

2) Presumably it appears to be human, not just "humanoid", or otherwise that would be noted.

3) If it maintains an consistent appearance, what are some other characteristics of its appearance, like race and sex?

SCP-1996 phenomenon consists of it appearing at historical or otherwise significant locations in the continental United States.

Does it only ever appear once at any particular location?

which was home to the third cousin of renown poet

The home to the third cousin of a renown poet isn't something I'd consider to be of any historical importance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Limiting damages is a valid means of containment… thing is, while this isn't dangerous, from the way the procedures are written, we have much less of a hand on this bugger than we have on even the Old Man.
It's a tourist who doesn't do anything harmful … yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've gone and rejiggered the containment procedures a bit.

Also, a big thank you to Djoric for really helping me flesh this one out in chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Containment sometimes means just managing the damage or effects as well as you can. The "standard disinformation campaign" line does feel like a cop out though.

I would've liked to have seen something like:

Any reports of SCP-1996 apparitions are to be handled by MTF-Kappa-56 ("Criss's Angels") using standard cover story "Street Performance".

instead. (Or maybe "Copper Field Mice".) The idea of a taskforce dedicated to saying "A magician did it" pleases me for reasons I'm unsure of.

I still liked it enough to upvote though. My favorite entry was the third cousin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I think that giving him a little more purpose, as reflected in the containment procedures, might be the extra kick this article needs. I mean, it's not bad, but by the end, I was kind of going, "Is that it?" I don't see any narrative thread or oomph, so I'll give this a "not bad" and no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is certainly a point.
That… That is what that is.

It's just that they like to say "Well, contain it this way…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feexed other oopsie

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This Euclid seems suspiciously like "uncontained" - asides from disinformation and getting the documents, we have no real idea about where he'll appear, why, how to contain him, what's his intent, what are his capabilities… I know containment means a wide range of things here, but this is stretching the definition in a newspeakish manner.

As an aside, is that the Carnival of Horrors in the first log? Either way, the last log is the only one that's understandable, and while someone using Foundation sites as an extradimensional tourist attraction is somewhat cute, it doesn't save the article.

EDIT: I was wondering why did the containment procedure seem familiar.

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-1234-J is to be contained in a containment chamber. This containment chamber is to contain precautions that will assist in the containment of SCP-1234-J. In the case of a containment breach personnel are to take necessary actions to contain SCP-1234-J. When personnel must enter SCP-1234's containment area, no fewer than a specified number may enter at any time. Personnel wishing to use SCP-1234-J for research purposes require a set clearance level in order to gain access.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I think pretty much every set of containment procedures on the wiki could be summarized like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the found documents are the most important part of something like this, and the few provided aren't strong enough to carry the article, IMO. The descriptions don't really read like most brochure copy, either (subject matter aside), they're more like a lazy parody of B-movie trailer narration you might expect to hear in a local TV spot. Promising premise, weak execution. Downvoting for now, but I'd be pleased to see an improved draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…Dance Machine forces you to all to boogie all night!"
Too many to's there. Didn't fix because I thought that slightly screwball grammer might be on purpose, what with being a tourist tout from outside reality and all.

Not bad, but I feel like I'm missing something. And not in the good "there's something else going on here" way. So, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It(s) all beautiful, isn't it?"

… Is that an its/it's mistake? I'm not sure …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Boom.

Reilly the Shadow Imp and The Dance Machine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to draft ghost-monkey the poop machine, but chat, thou art glorious in thy wisdom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude's a tourist. If that helps at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, it's a good read, but I just have to say.

What? It feels like the containment procedures could be summed up with "Well, contain it."
This threatens and confuses me.
No vote from me.
I don't like being confused >:U

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like saying this, but i dont get it. It feels like a weaker take on that one oceanic museum with anomalous people in it. And fish stuff. I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I challenge someone to come up with a good SCP for "Reilly the Shadow Imp" and/or "The Dance Machine".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I feel like the guy is a scout for an inter-dimensional tourist company. Amirite?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why do you post all the good ideas, Roget. Leave some ideas for the other kids.

For serious though, I thought at the beginning it was going to be 'Future Tourists with a Time Machine" and was gonna downvote. Then I got to the addendum, and I was 'dafuq' enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely not bad, but too much seems to me like something of a clone of all the "let's say creepy shit" SCPs we've had in the last couple of months. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feex

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-199 6will

Typo. Otherwise, it's really good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He's not the SCP, is he…

Upvoted.
